questions.stem: A team must extract organization-specific entity types that are not part of any built-in taxonomy, such as internal part numbers. Which capability should they use?

options.text: Prebuilt Named Entity Recognition
Custom Named Entity Recognition trained on labeled data
Key phrase extraction
Language detection

questions.explanation: Prebuilt NER recognizes only a fixed taxonomy of general categories. Custom NER lets you label examples of your own entity types and train a model to extract them.

questions.stem: A summarization feature must produce a novel, rephrased summary rather than reusing verbatim sentences from the source. Which mode is required?

options.text: Extractive summarization
Abstractive summarization
Key phrase extraction
Sentiment analysis

questions.explanation: Extractive summarization selects and ranks existing sentences, while abstractive summarization generates new wording that paraphrases the content. Only abstractive mode produces novel text.

questions.stem: A product team wants sentiment broken down by the specific aspect being discussed, such as "battery" versus "screen" in a review. What must be enabled?

options.text: Language detection
Opinion mining, by setting the show_opinion_mining flag
PII redaction
Extractive summarization

questions.explanation: Opinion mining, enabled with the show_opinion_mining flag, returns aspect (target) terms and the sentiment expressed toward each one, giving aspect-level rather than document-level sentiment.

questions.stem: Which statements about the PII detection and redaction feature are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: It returns a redacted_text field with detected entities masked
It can run in a Docker container for on-premises processing
It supports text, conversation, and document input shapes
It always uploads documents to a public endpoint with no container option

questions.explanation: PII detection returns redacted_text with entities masked, offers a container image for on-premises use, and supports text, conversation, and document inputs. The container option specifically avoids sending data to a cloud endpoint.

questions.stem: A translation workload processes millions of short strings and wants the most cost-effective option billed on character count. Which choice fits?

options.text: LLM-based translation billed per token
Neural Machine Translation through the Translator service, billed per character
The Speech translation SDK
Transliteration

questions.explanation: Translator's Neural Machine Translation is billed by characters and supports large per-request batches, making it economical for high-volume short strings. LLM-based translation is billed per token and has smaller request limits.

questions.stem: Transliteration converts text from one writing script to another while keeping the same language, rather than translating it into a different language.
questions.explanation: Transliteration maps characters between scripts, for example rendering Japanese text in Latin characters, without changing the underlying language. Translation, by contrast, changes the language itself.

questions.stem: Which capability-to-task mappings for the Azure AI Language and Translator services are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Named Entity Recognition to identify people, places, and organizations
Summarization to condense long documents
Translator to convert text into another language
Sentiment analysis to generate images from text

questions.explanation: NER identifies entities, summarization condenses text, and Translator converts between languages. Sentiment analysis scores emotional tone and does not generate images.

questions.stem: A desktop utility must transcribe a single short spoken command from the microphone using the Speech SDK. Which combination is correct?

options.text: A SpeechSynthesizer calling speak_text_async
A SpeechRecognizer configured with SpeechConfig and AudioConfig, calling recognize_once_async
The batch transcription REST API
A TranslationRecognizer with two target languages

questions.explanation: Real-time single-utterance transcription uses a SpeechRecognizer built from a SpeechConfig and an AudioConfig, invoking recognize_once_async. SpeechSynthesizer performs text-to-speech, and batch transcription is for large offline files.

questions.stem: Fabrikam Insurance must transcribe thousands of pre-recorded audio tracks pulled from claim video clips, running asynchronously overnight. Which approach is appropriate?

options.text: Real-time recognize_once_async in a loop
The batch transcription REST API
A SpeechSynthesizer
The text Translator service

questions.explanation: Batch transcription is a REST-based asynchronous service designed for large volumes of stored audio files, which fits an overnight bulk job. Real-time recognition is meant for live, interactive input.

questions.stem: A hospital wants higher recognition accuracy on specialized medical terms. After training a Custom Speech model, what is required to use it for real-time recognition?

options.text: Nothing; custom models apply automatically to the base endpoint
Deploy the model to a custom endpoint and reference its endpoint_id
Switch to the Translator service
Enable opinion mining

questions.explanation: A trained Custom Speech model must be deployed to a dedicated endpoint, and the application references that endpoint_id for real-time recognition. Word Error Rate is used to evaluate improvement before deployment.

questions.stem: A developer sends audio to a gpt-4o-audio-preview model using an input_audio content block and also wants the response validated against a JSON schema. What is the limitation?

options.text: input_audio blocks are not supported by audio models
Structured Outputs with json_schema is not supported for audio-preview models
Audio models cannot return any text
The audio must first be transcribed by Translator

questions.explanation: Audio-preview models accept input_audio content but do not support the Structured Outputs json_schema response format, so schema-validated output cannot be combined with audio input on those models.

questions.stem: Which statements about real-time and LLM-based speech options are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The GPT Realtime API uses a WebSocket connection for low-latency streaming
LLM Speech offers a multi-lingual default that recognizes across many languages
Some real-time translation models are billed hourly
Structured Outputs is guaranteed for every audio-preview model

questions.explanation: The GPT Realtime API streams over WebSocket, LLM Speech provides a multi-lingual default, and certain real-time translation models bill hourly. Structured Outputs is not supported on audio-preview models.

questions.stem: Using the Speech SDK for speech translation, a caller sets three target languages in a single request. What happens?

options.text: All three translations are returned normally
A single call is limited to two target languages; more requires a multi-service Foundry resource
Only transliteration is returned
The source language is auto-detected and the targets are ignored

questions.explanation: A standard speech translation call supports up to two target languages at once. Translating into more targets simultaneously requires a multi-service Foundry resource configuration.

questions.stem: A conference app must translate speech when the spoken source language is unknown and may change between speakers. Which capability fits?

options.text: A fixed source language on SpeechTranslationConfig
Live Interpreter, which auto-detects the source language
Transliteration
Extractive summarization

questions.explanation: Live Interpreter automatically detects the spoken source language, which suits multi-speaker sessions where the language is not known in advance. A fixed source language would fail when speakers switch languages.
